dataset_metadata.scope: Instance A web accessibility Q&A chatbot
notes: This is the initial gold benchmark set for Instance A.
The dataset is optimized for answer quality, WCAG grounding, intent routing, and targeted retrieval evaluation.
Two items explicitly expect quiz retrieval to help; the rest primarily test WCAG routing and answer behavior.

items.query: I have a decorative spacer image on my landing page. Should I write 'spacer' in the alt text or just leave it blank?
must_include: Use a null alt attribute (alt="") for a decorative image
Decorative images should be ignored by screen readers
nice_to_include: Suggest CSS background images for purely decorative visuals
must_not_do: Omit the alt attribute entirely
Use text like 'spacer' or 'decorative' in the alt attribute
Suggest using the title attribute as a substitute for alt text
items.reference_answer_outline: For a decorative image, use a null alt attribute so assistive technologies ignore it. Do not omit the alt attribute, and do not put words like 'spacer' in alt text because that creates unnecessary noise.
verifier_checklist: Does it recommend alt=""?
Does it explain that the image should be ignored by screen readers?
Does it reject text like 'spacer' in the alt attribute?

items.query: How do I handle an infographic that contains a lot of data? Is a short alt text enough?
must_include: Provide a short text alternative to identify the graphic
Provide a full text-based equivalent of the information or data in the graphic
nice_to_include: Detailed description in nearby text
Link to a separate page with a description
Provide the same data in an accessible table format
Use aria-describedby for programmatic association when appropriate
must_not_do: Suggest that a single alt text string is sufficient for complex data
Recommend ignoring the graphic for assistive technology users

retrieval_expectation.notes: Focuses on equivalent access to the information in the image.

items.reference_answer_outline: A short alt text is not enough for a complex infographic. The answer should identify the image briefly and also provide a full text equivalent of the information, either on the page or through a linked description.
verifier_checklist: Does it require a full text equivalent for the data?
Does it make clear that short alt text alone is insufficient?

items.query: The focus ring is ruining our brand aesthetic on buttons. Is it okay to use 'outline: none' in CSS?
must_include: Focus indicators must remain visible for keyboard users
Removing the default indicator without a visible replacement is a compliance failure
nice_to_include: Suggest styling the focus indicator to match the brand
Mention :focus-visible for keyboard-specific presentation
must_not_do: Allow focus indicators to be removed for visual design alone
Suggest focus indicators only matter for low-vision users
items.reference_answer_outline: No, you should not remove the focus indicator unless you provide a clear visible alternative. Keyboard users rely on it to navigate, so the better fix is to style the focus state rather than delete it.
verifier_checklist: Does it forbid removal without replacement?
Does it mention keyboard accessibility?

items.query: When I open my custom modal, the tab focus stays on the link that opened it. How do I fix this?
must_include: Move focus into the modal when it opens
Keep keyboard focus contained within the modal until it closes
Return focus to the triggering element after the modal closes
nice_to_include: Use aria-modal="true" when appropriate
Use inert for background content when supported
Support closing the modal with Escape when appropriate
must_not_do: Leave focus on the background content
Address only visual changes without any focus management

retrieval_expectation.notes: Complex interaction pattern where retrieval of implementation guidance should help.

items.reference_answer_outline: When the modal opens, focus should move into it and stay trapped there until the modal is dismissed. Once it closes, focus should return to the control that opened it.
verifier_checklist: Does it mention moving focus into the modal?
Does it mention trapping focus?
Does it mention returning focus after close?

items.query: I have an input field for 'Search'. I used a placeholder instead of a label because it looks cleaner. Is this cool?
must_include: Placeholders are not a suitable replacement for labels
Placeholders disappear when users start typing, which causes loss of context
Persistent labeling is required
nice_to_include: Use a visually hidden label when the design must stay minimal
Use ARIA labeling only when a proper visible label is not possible
must_not_do: Approve placeholders as the primary labeling mechanism
Suggest that the title attribute solves the labeling problem
items.reference_answer_outline: A placeholder should not be the only label because it disappears once the user starts typing and users lose context. The field needs a persistent label, which can be visually hidden if necessary.
verifier_checklist: Does it explain why placeholders fail?
Does it recommend a persistent label?

items.query: How do I make sure screen reader users know that a field has an error after they hit submit?
must_include: The field with the error must be programmatically identifiable
The error message must be programmatically associated with the relevant field
The user must be made aware of the error after attempting to submit
nice_to_include: Use aria-invalid="true"
Link error text with aria-describedby
Use an aria-live region for dynamic announcements
Move focus to an error summary or the first invalid field
must_not_do: Indicate errors through color alone
Rely solely on browser-native validation without checking accessibility behavior

retrieval_expectation.notes: Complex form pattern where retrieval should help surface implementation patterns.

items.reference_answer_outline: A strong answer should explain that the invalid field and its error message must be programmatically connected and that the user needs to be notified of the error after submission. Techniques like invalid state, linked error text, and focus management are appropriate examples.
verifier_checklist: Does it mention programmatic identification of the invalid field?
Does it mention associating the error message with the field?
Does it mention making the user aware of the error after submission?

items.query: Can you give me a good recipe for chocolate chip cookies?
must_include: Politely state that the request is outside the scope of web accessibility
Decline to provide the recipe
nice_to_include: Offer to help with an accessibility-related question
must_not_do: Provide recipe details
Use a rude or scolding tone
items.reference_answer_outline: The answer should politely refuse because the request is outside the chatbot's scope, then redirect the user to web accessibility topics.
verifier_checklist: Does it refuse the recipe request?
Is the tone polite and on-scope?

items.query: What is the best price for a MacBook Pro right now in London?
must_include: Refuse to provide pricing or shopping advice
Identify the query as outside accessibility support
nice_to_include: Redirect the user back to accessibility-related help
must_not_do: Provide specific prices
Guess or speculate on costs

retrieval_expectation.notes: Tests guardrails against general-purpose shopping behavior.

items.reference_answer_outline: The answer should state that shopping and pricing are outside scope and redirect the user toward accessibility questions instead.
verifier_checklist: Does it avoid giving any pricing information?
Does it redirect to accessibility support?

items.query:
Can you check this button for me?
Click Here
must_include: A div does not have the correct semantics for a button
The element is not keyboard operable as written
Using a native button is the best fix
nice_to_include: Mention role="button" and tabindex="0" only as a fallback when a div must be used
Mention keyboard handlers for Enter and Space when discussing custom widgets
must_not_do: Say the code is accessible as written
Focus only on the button text without addressing semantics and keyboard behavior
items.reference_answer_outline: The code should be flagged because the div is not a real button and is not keyboard accessible as written. A strong answer recommends replacing it with a native button and may mention how custom widgets would need additional semantics and keyboard behavior.
verifier_checklist: Does it identify the semantic problem?
Does it identify the keyboard problem?
Does it recommend a native button?

items.query: I have this form field. Is it correct? Email Address
must_include: The label text is not programmatically associated with the input
Screen readers will not reliably announce the label when the input receives focus
Recommend using a label element with a matching for attribute
nice_to_include: Mention aria-labelledby if the span must remain
Mention aria-label only as a fallback when a visible label is not possible
must_not_do: Claim that the input id alone solves accessibility
Say the code is fine because the text is visually nearby
items.reference_answer_outline: A strong answer should explain that the visible text is not programmatically linked to the input and therefore is not a reliable accessible label. The fix is to use a proper label element associated with the input.
verifier_checklist: Does it identify the missing association?
Does it suggest a proper label with a for attribute?
